[Cley next the Sea](/wiki/Cley_next_the_Sea "Cley next the Sea") and its [windmill](/wiki/Cley_Windmill "Cley Windmill"); [Norwich Cathedral](/wiki/Norwich_Cathedral "Norwich Cathedral"); and the [Guildhall](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn_Guildhall "King's Lynn Guildhall"), [King's Lynn](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn "King's Lynn"), showing Norfolk [flint](/wiki/Flint "Flint") [flushwork](/wiki/Flushwork "Flushwork") | |

([/ˈnɔːrfək, -fʊk/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*NOR-fək*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), [*-⁠fuuk*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England") in England, located in the [East of England](/wiki/East_of_England "East of England") and [East Anglia](/wiki/East_Anglia "East Anglia"). It borders [Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincolnshire "Lincolnshire") and [The Wash](/wiki/The_Wash "The Wash") to the north-west, the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") to the north and east, [Cambridgeshire](/wiki/Cambridgeshire "Cambridgeshire") to the west, and [Suffolk](/wiki/Suffolk "Suffolk") to the south. The largest settlement is the city of [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich").

**Norfolk** ([/ˈnɔːrfək, -fʊk/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*NOR-fək*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), [*-⁠fuuk*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England") in England, located in the [East of England](/wiki/East_of_England "East of England") and [East Anglia](/wiki/East_Anglia "East Anglia"). It borders [Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincolnshire "Lincolnshire") and [The Wash](/wiki/The_Wash "The Wash") to the north-west, the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") to the north and east, [Cambridgeshire](/wiki/Cambridgeshire "Cambridgeshire") to the west, and [Suffolk](/wiki/Suffolk "Suffolk") to the south. The largest settlement is the city of [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich").

The county has an area of 2,074 mi2 (5,370 km2) and had an estimated population of 940,359 in 2024. It is predominantly rural. Norwich is in the centre-east of the county, and its other principal settlements include [Great Yarmouth](/wiki/Great_Yarmouth "Great Yarmouth") on the east coast, [Thetford](/wiki/Thetford "Thetford") in the south, and [King's Lynn](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn "King's Lynn") in the north-west. For local government purposes Norfolk is a [non-metropolitan county](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_county "Non-metropolitan county") with seven districts.

The centre of Norfolk is gently undulating lowland. To the east are the northern half of [the Broads](/wiki/The_Broads "The Broads"), a network of rivers and lakes which extend into Suffolk and have a similar status to a [national park](/wiki/National_parks_of_England_and_Wales "National parks of England and Wales"). The west of the county contains part of [the Fens](/wiki/The_Fens "The Fens"), and to the south is part of [Thetford Forest](/wiki/Thetford_Forest "Thetford Forest"). The [geology](/wiki/Geology "Geology") of the county includes [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay") and [chalk](/wiki/Chalk "Chalk") deposits, which make its coast susceptible to [erosion](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion"); the [northern coast](/wiki/Norfolk_Coast_AONB "Norfolk Coast AONB") has been designated a [national landscape](/wiki/National_Landscape "National Landscape").

There is evidence of Prehistoric settlement in Norfolk. In the [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain") era the region was home to the [Iceni](/wiki/Iceni "Iceni"), whose leader [Boudica](/wiki/Boudica "Boudica") led a [major revolt](/wiki/Boudican_revolt "Boudican revolt") in AD60. The [Angles](/wiki/Angles_(tribe) "Angles (tribe)") settled the area in the 5th century, after which it became part of the [Kingdom of East Anglia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_East_Anglia "Kingdom of East Anglia"). During the later [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") the county was prosperous and heavily involved in the [wool trade](/wiki/Medieval_English_wool_trade "Medieval English wool trade"); this allowed the construction of [many large churches](/wiki/Wool_church "Wool church"). In 1549 Norfolk was the scene of [Kett's Rebellion](/wiki/Kett%27s_Rebellion "Kett's Rebellion"), which unsuccessfully protested the [enclosure](/wiki/Enclosure "Enclosure") of land. The county was not heavily industrialised during the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), and Norwich lost its status as one of England's largest cities. The contemporary economy is largely based on [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture") and tourism.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norfolk&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main articles: [Prehistoric Norfolk](/wiki/Prehistoric_Norfolk "Prehistoric Norfolk") and [History of Norfolk](/wiki/History_of_Norfolk "History of Norfolk")

The area that was to become Norfolk was settled in pre-Roman times (there were Palaeolithic settlers as early as 950,000 years ago), with camps along the higher land in the west, where [flints](/wiki/Flint "Flint") could be quarried.[[6]](#cite_note-6) A [Brittonic](/wiki/Celtic_Britons "Celtic Britons") tribe, the [Iceni](/wiki/Iceni "Iceni"), emerged in the 1st century [BC](/wiki/Anno_Domini "Anno Domini"). The Iceni revolted against the [Roman invasion](/wiki/Roman_invasion_of_Britain "Roman invasion of Britain") in AD 47, and again in 60 led by [Boudica](/wiki/Boudica "Boudica"). The crushing of the second rebellion opened the area to the Romans. During the [Roman era](/wiki/Roman_Norfolk "Roman Norfolk") roads and ports were constructed throughout the area and farming was widespread.

Situated on the east coast, the homelands of the Iceni were vulnerable to attacks from continental Europe and other parts of Britain, and forts were built to defend against raids by the [Saxons](/wiki/Saxons "Saxons") and the [Picts](/wiki/Picts "Picts"). A period of depopulation, which may have been due to these threats, seems to have followed the departure of the Romans.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Soon afterward, Germanic peoples from the North Sea area settled in the region. Though they became known as [Angles](/wiki/Angles_(tribe) "Angles (tribe)"), they were likely not affiliated to any tribe in particular at the time of their migration. It is thought that the settlement here was early (possibly beginning at the start of the fifth century, thereby preceding the alleged date of [Hengist and Horsa](/wiki/Hengist_and_Horsa "Hengist and Horsa")'s arrival in Kent) and that it occurred on a large scale.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

By the 5th century, the Angles had established control over the region.[[11]](#cite_note-11) They later became known as the "north folk" and the "south folk," giving rise to the names "Norfolk" and "Suffolk."[[12]](#cite_note-12) Norfolk, Suffolk, and nearby territories formed the Kingdom of East Anglia, one of the Anglo-Saxon heptarchy, which was later incorporated into [Mercia](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia") and subsequently into [Wessex](/wiki/Wessex "Wessex").[[13]](#cite_note-13) The influence of the early English settlers is evident in numerous place names ending in "-ham," "-ingham," and "-ton."[[14]](#cite_note-14) Place-name endings such as "-by" and "-thorpe" are also common and indicate Danish origins; during the 9th century, the region was subject to Danish attacks, during which King [Edmund the Martyr](/wiki/Edmund_the_Martyr "Edmund the Martyr") was killed.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Additionally, several place names in the Fenland area contain Celtic elements,[[16]](#cite_note-celtic-16) which some scholars interpret as evidence of a significant Brittonic population in the region.[[16]](#cite_note-celtic-16)

In the centuries before the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest") the wetlands of the east of the county began to be converted to farmland, and settlements grew in these areas. Migration into East Anglia must have been high: by the time of the [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book") survey it was one of the most densely populated parts of the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"). During the high and late [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") the county developed arable agriculture and woollen industries. Norfolk's prosperity at that time is evident from the county's large number of medieval churches: out of an original total of over one thousand some 659 have survived, more than in any other county in Britain and the greatest concentration in the world.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The economy was in decline by the time of the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death"), which dramatically reduced the population in 1349.

[Kett's Rebellion](/wiki/Kett%27s_Rebellion "Kett's Rebellion") occurred in Norfolk during the reign of [Edward VI](/wiki/Edward_VI "Edward VI"), largely in response to the enclosure of land by landlords, leaving peasants with nowhere to graze their animals, and to the general abuses of power by the nobility. It was led by [Robert Kett](/wiki/Robert_Kett "Robert Kett"), a yeoman farmer, who was joined by recruits from Norwich and the surrounding countryside. His group numbered some 16,000 by the time the rebels stormed Norwich on 29 July 1549 and took the city. Kett's rebellion ended on 27 August when the rebels were defeated by an army under the leadership of [John Dudley, 1st Duke of Northumberland](/wiki/John_Dudley,_1st_Duke_of_Northumberland "John Dudley, 1st Duke of Northumberland") at the Battle of Dussindale. Some 3,000 rebels were killed. Kett was captured, held in the Tower of London, tried for treason, and hanged from the walls of Norwich Castle.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

By the late 16th century Norwich had grown to become the second-largest city in England, but over one-third of its population died in the [plague](/wiki/Plague_(disease) "Plague (disease)") epidemic of 1579,[[21]](#cite_note-21) and in 1665 the [Great Plague](/wiki/Great_Plague_of_London "Great Plague of London") again killed around one-third of the population.[[22]](#cite_note-22) During the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") Norfolk was largely [Parliamentarian](/wiki/Roundheads "Roundheads"). The economy and agriculture of the region declined somewhat. During the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") Norfolk developed little industry, except in Norwich, which was a late addition to the railway network.

Early military units included the [Norfolk Militia](/wiki/Norfolk_Militia "Norfolk Militia"). The local [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") regiments included the [Royal Norfolk Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Norfolk_Regiment "Royal Norfolk Regiment") (now the [Royal Anglian Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Anglian_Regiment "Royal Anglian Regiment")) and the [Norfolk Yeomanry](/wiki/Norfolk_Yeomanry "Norfolk Yeomanry"). In the 20th century the county developed a role in aviation. The first development in airfields came with the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"); there was then a massive expansion during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") with the growth of the [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force") and the influx of the American USAAF [8th Air Force](/wiki/8th_Air_Force "8th Air Force") which operated from many [Norfolk airfields](/wiki/List_of_Norfolk_airfields "List of Norfolk airfields").

During the Second World War agriculture rapidly intensified, and it has remained very intensive since, with the establishment of large fields for growing cereals and [oilseed rape](/wiki/Rapeseed "Rapeseed").

## Economy and industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norfolk&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Economy and industry")]

In 1998 Norfolk had a [Gross Domestic Product](/wiki/Gross_Domestic_Product "Gross Domestic Product") of [£](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling")9,319 million, which represents 1.5% of England's economy and 1.25% of the United Kingdom's economy. The GDP per head was £11,825, compared to £13,635 for East Anglia, £12,845 for England and £12,438 for the United Kingdom. In 1999–2000 the county had an unemployment rate of 5.6%, compared to 5.8% for England and 6.0% for the UK.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Data from 2017 provided a useful update on the county's economy. The median hourly gross pay was £12.17 and the median weekly pay was £496.80; on a per year basis, the median gross income was £25,458. The employment rate among persons aged 16 to 64 was 74.2% while the unemployment rate was 4.6%.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The Norfolk economy was "treading water with manufacturing sales and recruitment remaining static in the first quarter of the year" according to research published in April 2018. A spokesperson for the
Norfolk Chamber of Commerce made this comment: "At a time when Norfolk firms face steep up-front costs, the apprenticeship system is in crisis, roads are being allowed to crumble, mobile phone and broadband 'not-spots' are multiplying, it's obvious that the key to improved productivity and competitiveness lies in getting the basics right". The solution was seen as a need for the UK government to provide "a far stronger domestic economic agenda ... to fix the fundamentals needed for business to thrive here..."[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In 2017, tourism was adding £3.25 billion to the economy per year and supported some 65,000 jobs, being the fifth most important employment in Norfolk. The visitor economy had increased in value by more than £500 million since 2012.
[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Important business sectors also include energy (oil, gas and renewables), advanced engineering and manufacturing, and food and farming.

Much of Norfolk's fairly flat and fertile land has been drained for use as [arable land](/wiki/Arable_land "Arable land"). The principal arable crops are [sugar beet](/wiki/Sugar_beet "Sugar beet"), wheat, [barley](/wiki/Barley "Barley") (for brewing) and [oil seed rape](/wiki/Oil_seed_rape "Oil seed rape"). The county also boasts a [saffron](/wiki/Saffron "Saffron") grower.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Over 20% of employment in the county is in the agricultural and food industries.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Well-known companies in Norfolk are [Aviva](/wiki/Aviva "Aviva") (formerly [Norwich Union](/wiki/Norwich_Union "Norwich Union")), [Colman's](/wiki/Colman%27s "Colman's") (part of [Unilever](/wiki/Unilever "Unilever")), [Lotus Cars](/wiki/Lotus_Cars "Lotus Cars") and [Bernard Matthews Farms](/wiki/Bernard_Matthews_Farms "Bernard Matthews Farms"). The [Construction Industry Training Board](/wiki/Construction_Industry_Training_Board "Construction Industry Training Board") is based on the former airfield of [RAF Bircham Newton](/wiki/RAF_Bircham_Newton "RAF Bircham Newton"). Brewer [Greene King](/wiki/Greene_King "Greene King"), food producer [Cranswick](/wiki/Cranswick_plc "Cranswick plc") and feed supplier [ForFarmers](/w/index.php?title=ForFarmers&action=edit&redlink=1 "ForFarmers (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/ForFarmers "nl:ForFarmers")] were seeing growth in 2016–2017.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

A [local enterprise partnership](/wiki/Local_enterprise_partnership "Local enterprise partnership") was being established by business leaders to help grow jobs across Norfolk and Suffolk. They secured an [enterprise zone](/wiki/Enterprise_zone "Enterprise zone") to help grow businesses in the energy sector, and established the two counties as a centre for growing services and products for the [green economy](/wiki/Green_economy "Green economy").

To help local industry in Norwich, the local council offered a wireless internet service, but this was subsequently withdrawn as funding had ceased.[[30]](#cite_note-archive1-30)

The fishery business still continued in 2018, with individuals such as John Lee, a fifth generation crabman, who sells Cromer Crabs to eateries such as M Restaurants and the Blueprint Café. The problem that he has found is attracting young people to this small industry which calls for working many hours per week during the season.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Lobster trapping also continued in North Norfolk, around [Sheringham](/wiki/Sheringham "Sheringham") and [Cromer](/wiki/Cromer "Cromer"), for example.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Management of the shoreline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norfolk&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Management of the shoreline")]

Norfolk's low-lying land and easily eroded cliffs, many of which are composed of chalk and clay, make it vulnerable to weathering by the sea. The most recent major erosion event occurred during the [North Sea flood of 1953](/wiki/North_Sea_flood_of_1953 "North Sea flood of 1953").

The low-lying section of coast between [Kelling](/wiki/Kelling "Kelling") and [Lowestoft Ness](/wiki/Lowestoft_Ness "Lowestoft Ness") in Suffolk is currently managed by the British [Environment Agency](/wiki/Environment_Agency "Environment Agency") to protect the Broads from sea flooding. Management policy for the North Norfolk coastline is described in the "North Norfolk Shoreline Management Plan" published in 2006, but has yet[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] to be accepted by local authorities.[[33]](#cite_note-33) The Shoreline Management Plan states that the stretch of coast will be protected for at least another 50 years, but that in the event of [sea level rise](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise") and [post-glacial lowering](/wiki/Post-glacial_rebound "Post-glacial rebound") of land levels in the South East, there may a need for further research to inform future management decisions, including the possibility that the [sea defences](/wiki/Coastal_management "Coastal management") may have to be [realigned](/wiki/Managed_retreat "Managed retreat") to a more sustainable position. [Natural England](/wiki/Natural_England "Natural England") have contributed some research into the impacts on the environment of various realignment options. The draft report of their research was leaked to the press, who created great anxiety by reporting that Natural England plan to abandon a large section of the Norfolk Broads, villages and farmland to the sea to save the rest of the Norfolk coastline from the impact of any adverse [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change").[[34]](#cite_note-34)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norfolk&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Media")]

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norfolk&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Television")]

The county is covered by [BBC East](/wiki/BBC_East "BBC East") and [ITV Anglia](/wiki/ITV_Anglia "ITV Anglia"), which both broadcast from Norwich. Television signals are received from the [Tacolneston](/wiki/Tacolneston_transmitting_station "Tacolneston transmitting station") TV transmitter.[[35]](#cite_note-35) However, northwestern parts of Norfolk including [King's Lynn](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn "King's Lynn"), [Hunstanton](/wiki/Hunstanton "Hunstanton") and [Wells-next-the-Sea](/wiki/Wells-next-the-Sea "Wells-next-the-Sea") are covered by [BBC Yorkshire and Lincolnshire](/wiki/BBC_Yorkshire_and_Lincolnshire "BBC Yorkshire and Lincolnshire"), broadcasting from [Hull](/wiki/Kingston_upon_Hull "Kingston upon Hull"), and [ITV Yorkshire](/wiki/ITV_Yorkshire "ITV Yorkshire"), which broadcast from [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds"). The area receives its television signals from the [Belmont](/wiki/Belmont_transmitting_station "Belmont transmitting station") TV transmitter.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

### Radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norfolk&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Radio")]

[BBC Local Radio](/wiki/BBC_Local_Radio "BBC Local Radio") for the county is served by [BBC Radio Norfolk](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Norfolk "BBC Radio Norfolk"). County-wide commercial radio stations are [Heart East](/wiki/Heart_East "Heart East"), [Greatest Hits Radio East](/wiki/Greatest_Hits_Radio_East "Greatest Hits Radio East"), [Amber Radio](/wiki/Amber_Radio "Amber Radio"), and [Kiss](/wiki/Kiss_105-108 "Kiss 105-108"). Community based stations are [Future Radio](/wiki/Future_Radio "Future Radio") (serving Norwich), Harbour Radio (for [Great Yarmouth](/wiki/Great_Yarmouth "Great Yarmouth")),[[37]](#cite_note-37) KL1 Radio (covering [North West Norfolk](/wiki/North_West_Norfolk "North West Norfolk")) [[38]](#cite_note-38) and Poppyland Community Radio (serving [North Norfolk](/wiki/North_Norfolk "North Norfolk")).[[39]](#cite_note-39)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norfolk&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Education")]

### Primary and secondary education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norfolk&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Primary and secondary education")]

See also: [List of schools in Norfolk](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Norfolk "List of schools in Norfolk")

Before 2011, Norfolk had a completely [comprehensive state education](/wiki/Comprehensive_school "Comprehensive school") or "maintained" system managed by [Norfolk County Council](/wiki/Norfolk_County_Council "Norfolk County Council"), with secondary school age from 11 to 16 or in some schools with [sixth forms](/wiki/Sixth_form "Sixth form"), 18 years old.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Since then, a number of schools formerly in the "maintained" system have left it to become [academies](/wiki/Academy_(English_school) "Academy (English school)"), or members of academy groups. Others have become [free schools](/wiki/Free_school_(England) "Free school (England)"). Both academies and free schools are still publicly funded by the Department of Education but are not with county council management.

In many of the rural areas, there is no nearby sixth form, and so [sixth form colleges](/wiki/Sixth_form_college "Sixth form college") are found in larger towns. There are twelve [private](/wiki/Private_schools_in_the_United_Kingdom "Private schools in the United Kingdom"), or private schools, including [Gresham's School](/wiki/Gresham%27s_School "Gresham's School") in [Holt](/wiki/Holt,_Norfolk "Holt, Norfolk") in the north of the county, [Thetford Grammar School](/wiki/Thetford_Grammar_School "Thetford Grammar School") in [Thetford](/wiki/Thetford "Thetford"), which is [Britain's fifth oldest extant school](/wiki/List_of_the_oldest_schools_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of the oldest schools in the United Kingdom"), [Langley School](/wiki/Langley_School,_Loddon "Langley School, Loddon") in [Loddon](/wiki/Loddon,_Norfolk "Loddon, Norfolk"), and several in the city of Norwich, including [Norwich School](/wiki/Norwich_School "Norwich School") and [Norwich High School for Girls](/wiki/Norwich_High_School_for_Girls "Norwich High School for Girls"). The King's Lynn district has the largest school population. Norfolk is also home to [Wymondham College](/wiki/Wymondham_College "Wymondham College"), the UK's largest remaining state [boarding school](/wiki/Boarding_school "Boarding school").

### Tertiary education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norfolk&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Tertiary education")]

The [University of East Anglia](/wiki/University_of_East_Anglia "University of East Anglia") is located on the outskirts of Norwich, and [Norwich University of the Arts](/wiki/Norwich_University_of_the_Arts "Norwich University of the Arts") is based in seven buildings in and around St George's Street in the city centre, next to the [River Wensum](/wiki/River_Wensum "River Wensum").

The [City College Norwich](/wiki/City_College_Norwich "City College Norwich") and the [College of West Anglia](/wiki/College_of_West_Anglia "College of West Anglia") are colleges covering Norwich and King's Lynn as well as Norfolk as a whole. [Easton & Otley College](/wiki/Easton_%26_Otley_College "Easton & Otley College"), 7 mi (11 km) west of Norwich, provides agriculture-based courses for the county, parts of [Suffolk](/wiki/Suffolk "Suffolk"), and nationally.

The [University of Suffolk](/wiki/University_of_Suffolk "University of Suffolk") also runs higher education courses in Norfolk, from multiple locations including [Great Yarmouth College](/wiki/Great_Yarmouth_College "Great Yarmouth College").[[30]](#cite_note-archive1-30)

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norfolk&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Politics")]

### Local

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norfolk&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Local")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Norfolk_UK_local_election_results_2011_map.svg/250px-Norfolk_UK_local_election_results_2011_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Norfolk_UK_local_election_results_2011_map.svg)

Ward-by-ward map of the 2011 local district election results

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Norfolk_County_Council_election_2013_map.svg/250px-Norfolk_County_Council_election_2013_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Norfolk_County_Council_election_2013_map.svg)

Map of the 2013 Norfolk County Council election results

Norfolk is administered by Norfolk County Council, which is the top tier local government authority, based at County Hall in Norwich. For details of the authority click on the link [Norfolk County Council](/wiki/Norfolk_County_Council "Norfolk County Council").

Below Norfolk County Council the county is divided into seven second tier district councils: [Breckland District](/wiki/Breckland_(district) "Breckland (district)"), [Broadland District](/wiki/Broadland "Broadland"), [Great Yarmouth Borough](/wiki/Great_Yarmouth_(borough) "Great Yarmouth (borough)"), [King's Lynn and West Norfolk Borough](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn_and_West_Norfolk "King's Lynn and West Norfolk"), [North Norfolk District](/wiki/North_Norfolk "North Norfolk"), [Norwich City](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich") and [South Norfolk District](/wiki/South_Norfolk "South Norfolk").

Below the second tier councils the majority of the county is divided into parish and town councils, the lowest tier of local government (the only exceptions being parts of Norwich and King's Lynn urban areas).

Currently the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") control five of the seven district councils: [Breckland District](/wiki/Breckland_District "Breckland District"), [Broadland District](/wiki/Broadland "Broadland"), [King's Lynn and West Norfolk Borough](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn_and_West_Norfolk "King's Lynn and West Norfolk"), [Great Yarmouth Borough](/wiki/Borough_of_Great_Yarmouth "Borough of Great Yarmouth") and [South Norfolk District](/wiki/South_Norfolk_District "South Norfolk District") while [Norwich City](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich") is controlled by the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") and [North Norfolk District](/wiki/North_Norfolk "North Norfolk") by the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)").

[Norfolk County Council](/wiki/Norfolk_County_Council "Norfolk County Council") has been under [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") control since 2017. There have been two periods when the council has not been run by the Conservative Party, both when no party had overall control, these were 1993–2001 and 2013–2017.

In the 2010 General Election seven seats were held by the [Conservatives](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") and two by the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)"). The [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") no longer held the urban constituencies they once held in Norwich North and Great Yarmouth, leaving them with no MP's in the whole of [East Anglia](/wiki/East_Anglia "East Anglia"); the former Labour [Home Secretary](/wiki/Home_Secretary "Home Secretary") [Charles Clarke](/wiki/Charles_Clarke "Charles Clarke") was a high level casualty of that election.

In the 2015 General Election seven seats were won by the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)"), with [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") winning Norwich South and the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)") winning North Norfolk.

In the 2017 General Election the 2015 result was repeated.

In the 2024 General Election, Norfolk became the only county in the United Kingdom to be represented by MPs from five different parties.

### Norwich Unitary Authority dispute (2006–2010)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norfolk&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Norwich Unitary Authority dispute (2006–2010)")]

In October 2006, the [Department for Communities and Local Government](/wiki/Department_for_Communities_and_Local_Government "Department for Communities and Local Government") produced a Local Government White Paper inviting councils to submit proposals for unitary restructuring. In January 2007 Norwich submitted its proposal, but this was rejected in December 2007 as it did not meet the criteria for acceptance. In February 2008, the [Boundary Committee for England](/wiki/Boundary_Committee_for_England "Boundary Committee for England") (from 1 April 2010 incorporated in the [Local Government Boundary Commission for England](/wiki/Local_Government_Boundary_Commission_for_England "Local Government Boundary Commission for England")) was asked to consider alternative proposals for the whole or part of Norfolk, including whether Norwich should become a [unitary authority](/wiki/Unitary_authority "Unitary authority"), separate from Norfolk County Council. In December 2009, the Boundary Committee recommended a single unitary authority covering all of Norfolk, including Norwich.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)

However, on 10 February 2010, it was announced that, contrary to the December 2009 recommendation of the Boundary Committee, Norwich would be given separate unitary status.[[45]](#cite_note-45) The proposed change was strongly resisted, principally by Norfolk County Council and the Conservative opposition in Parliament.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Reacting to the announcement, Norfolk County Council issued a statement that it would seek leave to challenge the decision in the courts.[[47]](#cite_note-47) A letter was leaked to the local media in which the Permanent Secretary for the Department for Communities and Local Government noted that the decision did not meet all the criteria and that the risk of it "being successfully challenged in judicial review proceedings is very high".[[48]](#cite_note-48) The Shadow Local Government and Planning Minister, [Bob Neill](/wiki/Bob_Neill "Bob Neill"), stated that should the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") win the [2010 general election](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election"), they would reverse the decision.[[49]](#cite_note-edp2feb-49)

Following the [2010 general election](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election"), [Eric Pickles](/wiki/Eric_Jack_Pickles "Eric Jack Pickles") was appointed [Secretary of State for Communities and Local Government](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Communities_and_Local_Government "Secretary of State for Communities and Local Government") on 12 May 2010 in a [Conservative–Liberal Democrat coalition government](/wiki/Cameron%E2%80%93Clegg_coalition "Cameron–Clegg coalition"). According to press reports, he instructed his department to take urgent steps to reverse the decision and maintain the status quo in line with the Conservative Party manifesto.[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51) However, the unitary plans were supported by the Liberal Democrat group on the city council, and by [Simon Wright](/wiki/Simon_Wright_(politician) "Simon Wright (politician)"), LibDem MP for [Norwich South](/wiki/Norwich_South_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Norwich South (UK Parliament constituency)"), who intended to lobby the party leadership to allow the changes to go ahead.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

The [Local Government Act 2010](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_2010 "Local Government Act 2010") to reverse the unitary decision for Norwich (and Exeter and Suffolk) received Royal Assent on 16 December 2010. The disputed award of unitary status had meanwhile been referred to the [High Court](/wiki/High_Court_of_Justice "High Court of Justice"), and on 21 June 2010 the court ([Mr. Justice Ouseley](/wiki/Mr._Justice_Ouseley "Mr. Justice Ouseley"), judge) ruled it unlawful, and revoked it. The city has therefore failed to attain unitary status, and the two-tier arrangement of County and District Councils (with Norwich City Council counted among the latter) remains as of 2017.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

See also: [List of places in Norfolk](/wiki/List_of_places_in_Norfolk "List of places in Norfolk") and [List of settlements in Norfolk by population](/wiki/List_of_settlements_in_Norfolk_by_population "List of settlements in Norfolk by population")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Flag_of_Norwich.svg/250px-Flag_of_Norwich.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Norwich.svg)

Flag of Norwich

Norfolk's county town and only [city](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom") is [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich"), one of the largest settlements in England during the [Norman era](/wiki/Normans "Normans"). Norwich is home to the [University of East Anglia](/wiki/University_of_East_Anglia "University of East Anglia"), and is the county's main business and culture centre. Other principal towns include the port town of [King's Lynn](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn "King's Lynn") and the seaside resort and Broads gateway town of [Great Yarmouth](/wiki/Great_Yarmouth "Great Yarmouth").

Based on the 2011 Census[[54]](#cite_note-BUA-54) the county's largest centres of population are:
[Norwich](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich") (213,166),
[Great Yarmouth](/wiki/Great_Yarmouth "Great Yarmouth") (63,434),
[King's Lynn](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn "King's Lynn") (46,093),
[Thetford](/wiki/Thetford,_Norfolk "Thetford, Norfolk") (24,883),
[Dereham](/wiki/Dereham "Dereham") (20,651),
[Wymondham](/wiki/Wymondham "Wymondham") (13,587),
[North Walsham](/wiki/North_Walsham "North Walsham") (12,463),
[Attleborough](/wiki/Attleborough "Attleborough") (10,549),
[Downham Market](/wiki/Downham_Market "Downham Market") (9,994),
[Diss](/wiki/Diss,_Norfolk "Diss, Norfolk") (9,829),
[Fakenham](/wiki/Fakenham "Fakenham") (8,285),
[Cromer](/wiki/Cromer "Cromer") (7,749),
[Sheringham](/wiki/Sheringham "Sheringham") (7,367)
and [Swaffham](/wiki/Swaffham "Swaffham") (7,258).
There are also several smaller [market towns](/wiki/Market_town "Market town"): [Aylsham](/wiki/Aylsham "Aylsham") (6,016), [Harleston](/wiki/Harleston,_Norfolk "Harleston, Norfolk") (4,458) and [Holt](/wiki/Holt,_Norfolk "Holt, Norfolk") (3,810).

Much of the county remains rural in nature and Norfolk is believed to have around [200 lost settlements](/wiki/List_of_lost_settlements_in_Norfolk "List of lost settlements in Norfolk") which have been largely or totally depopulated since the medieval period. These include places lost to coastal erosion, agricultural [enclosure](/wiki/Enclosure "Enclosure"), depopulation and the establishment of the [Stanford Training Area](/wiki/Stanford_Training_Area "Stanford Training Area") in 1940.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norfolk&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Transport")]

### Roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norfolk&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Roads")]

Norfolk is one of the few counties in England that does not have a motorway. The [A11](/wiki/A11_road_(Great_Britain) "A11 road (Great Britain)") connects Norfolk to [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge "Cambridge") and London, via the [M11](/wiki/M11_motorway_(Great_Britain) "M11 motorway (Great Britain)"). From the west, there are only two routes from Norfolk that provide a direct link with the [A1](/wiki/A1_road_(Great_Britain) "A1 road (Great Britain)"): the [A47](/wiki/A47_road_(Great_Britain) "A47 road (Great Britain)") to the [East Midlands](/wiki/East_Midlands "East Midlands") and [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), via [Peterborough](/wiki/Peterborough,_Cambridgeshire "Peterborough, Cambridgeshire"), and the [A17](/wiki/A17_road_(Great_Britain) "A17 road (Great Britain)") to the East Midlands, via [Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincolnshire "Lincolnshire"). These two routes both meet at [King's Lynn](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn "King's Lynn"), which is also the starting point of the [A10](/wiki/A10_road_(Great_Britain) "A10 road (Great Britain)"), providing West Norfolk with a direct link with London, via [Ely](/wiki/Ely,_Cambridgeshire "Ely, Cambridgeshire"), Cambridge and [Hertford](/wiki/Hertford "Hertford").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Norwich_Railway_Station_Platforms.jpg/250px-Norwich_Railway_Station_Platforms.jpg)](/wiki/File:Norwich_Railway_Station_Platforms.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Holt_railway_station_heritage_2022.jpg/250px-Holt_railway_station_heritage_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Holt_railway_station_heritage_2022.jpg)

[Holt railway station](/wiki/Holt_railway_station_(North_Norfolk_Railway) "Holt railway station (North Norfolk Railway)")

There are two main railway lines that link Norfolk with London. The [Great Eastern Main Line](/wiki/Great_Eastern_Main_Line "Great Eastern Main Line") hosts inter-city services from [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich_railway_station "Norwich railway station") to [Liverpool Street](/wiki/Liverpool_Street_Station "Liverpool Street Station"), via [Ipswich](/wiki/Ipswich_railway_station "Ipswich railway station") and [Colchester](/wiki/Colchester_railway_station "Colchester railway station"). The [Fen line](/wiki/Fen_line "Fen line") provides regular services between [King's Lynn](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn_railway_station "King's Lynn railway station") and [King's Cross](/wiki/London_King%27s_Cross_railway_station "London King's Cross railway station"), via [Ely](/wiki/Ely_railway_station "Ely railway station") and [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge_railway_station "Cambridge railway station").

In addition, the [Breckland line](/wiki/Breckland_line "Breckland line") provides access from Norwich and [Thetford](/wiki/Thetford_railway_station "Thetford railway station") to destinations to the west including [Peterborough](/wiki/Peterborough_railway_station "Peterborough railway station"), [Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham_railway_station "Nottingham railway station"), [Sheffield](/wiki/Sheffield_railway_station "Sheffield railway station"), [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester_Piccadilly_railway_station "Manchester Piccadilly railway station") and [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_Lime_Street_railway_station "Liverpool Lime Street railway station").

## Dialect, accent and nickname

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norfolk&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Dialect, accent and nickname")]

Main article: [Norfolk dialect](/wiki/Norfolk_dialect "Norfolk dialect")

The Norfolk dialect, sometimes referred to as "Broad Norfolk," has gradually declined over the modern era. Much of its distinctive vocabulary and many traditional phrases have disappeared due to factors such as the influence of radio, television, and migration from other parts of the country. Today, Norfolk speech is often regarded more as a regional [accent](/wiki/Accent_(dialect) "Accent (dialect)") than a separate [dialect](/wiki/Dialect "Dialect"), although it retains some distinctive grammatical features.[[55]](#cite_note-55)
[[56]](#cite_note-56)

People from Norfolk are sometimes known as [Norfolk Dumplings](/wiki/Dumpling "Dumpling"),[[57]](#cite_note-57) an allusion to the flour dumplings that were traditionally a significant part of the local diet.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

More cutting, perhaps, was the alleged pejorative [medical slang](/wiki/Medical_slang "Medical slang") term "Normal for Norfolk",[[59]](#cite_note-59) alluding to the county's perceived status as a quirky rustic backwater due to a high level of [inbreeding](/wiki/Inbreeding "Inbreeding") among residents.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)

## Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norfolk&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Norfolk is a popular tourist destination and has several major holiday attractions. There are many seaside resorts, including some of the finest British beaches, such as those at [Great Yarmouth](/wiki/Great_Yarmouth "Great Yarmouth"), [Cromer](/wiki/Cromer "Cromer") and [Holkham](/wiki/Holkham "Holkham"). Norfolk contains [the Broads](/wiki/The_Broads "The Broads") and other areas of outstanding [natural](/wiki/Natural_history "Natural history") beauty and many areas of the coast are wild bird sanctuaries and reserves with some areas designated as [national parks](/wiki/National_parks_of_England_and_Wales "National parks of England and Wales") such as the [Norfolk Coast AONB](/wiki/Norfolk_Coast_AONB "Norfolk Coast AONB").

* [![The bridge at Wroxham](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/WroxhamBridge.jpg/250px-WroxhamBridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:WroxhamBridge.jpg "The bridge at Wroxham")

  The beach at [Holkham National Nature Reserve](/wiki/Holkham_National_Nature_Reserve "Holkham National Nature Reserve")

The [King](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III")'s residence at [Sandringham House](/wiki/Sandringham_House "Sandringham House") in [Sandringham](/wiki/Sandringham,_Norfolk "Sandringham, Norfolk") provides a year-round tourist attraction whilst the coast and some rural areas are popular locations for people from the [conurbations](/wiki/Conurbation "Conurbation") to purchase weekend [holiday homes](/wiki/Vacation_property "Vacation property"). [Arthur Conan Doyle](/wiki/Arthur_Conan_Doyle "Arthur Conan Doyle") first conceived the idea for *[The Hound of the Baskervilles](/wiki/The_Hound_of_the_Baskervilles "The Hound of the Baskervilles")* whilst holidaying in [Cromer](/wiki/Cromer "Cromer") with [Bertram Fletcher Robinson](/wiki/Bertram_Fletcher_Robinson "Bertram Fletcher Robinson"), after hearing local folklore tales regarding the mysterious hound known as [Black Shuck](/wiki/Black_Shuck "Black Shuck").[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)

### Amusement parks and zoos

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norfolk&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Amusement parks and zoos")]

Norfolk has several amusement parks and zoos.

* Thrigby Hall near Great Yarmouth was built in 1736 by Joshua Smith Esquire and features a zoo which houses a large tiger enclosure, primate enclosures and the swamp house which has many crocodiles and alligators.
* [Holkham Hall](/wiki/Holkham_Hall "Holkham Hall") is an 18th-century stately home and [visitor attraction](/wiki/Tourist_attraction "Tourist attraction"), constructed in the [Palladian](/wiki/Palladian_architecture "Palladian architecture") style and at the centre of a 3,000-acre deer park on the [North Norfolk](/wiki/North_Norfolk "North Norfolk") coast with a woodland play area, walled garden and farming exhibition.
* Roarr! Dinosaur Adventure (formerly Dinosaur Adventure) is a [dinosaur](/wiki/Dinosaur "Dinosaur") themed adventure park in [Lenwade](/wiki/Lenwade "Lenwade"). It is set in 85 acres of parkland and has a dinosaur trail, indoor play area, high ropes course and outdoor water play area.
* [Great Yarmouth Pleasure Beach](/wiki/Great_Yarmouth_Pleasure_Beach "Great Yarmouth Pleasure Beach") is a free-entry theme park, hosting over twenty large rides as well as a crazy golf course, water attractions, children's rides and "white knuckle" rides.
* [BeWILDerwood](/wiki/BeWILDerwood "BeWILDerwood") is an adventure park situated in the Norfolk Broads and is the setting for the book *A Boggle at BeWILDerwood* by local children's author [Tom Blofeld](/wiki/Tom_Blofeld "Tom Blofeld").
* Britannia Pier on the coast of Great Yarmouth has rides which include a ghost train. Also on the pier is the famous Britannia Pier Theatre.
* [Banham Zoo](/wiki/Banham_Zoo "Banham Zoo") is set amongst 35 acres (14 ha) of parkland and gardens with enclosures for animals including big cats, birds of prey, siamangs and shire horses. Its annual visitor attendance is in excess of 200,000 people.[[64]](#cite_note-64)
* [Pensthorpe Nature Reserve](/wiki/Pensthorpe_Nature_Reserve "Pensthorpe Nature Reserve"), near the town of Fakenham in north Norfolk, is a nature reserve with many captive birds and animals. Such species include native birds such as lapwing and Eurasian crane, to much more exotic examples like Marabou stork, Greater flamingo, and Manchurian crane. The site played host to the BBC's *Springwatch* from 2008 until 2010. A number of human-made lakes are home to a range of wild birds, and provide stop-off points for many wintering ducks and geese.
* The [Sea Life Centre](/wiki/Sea_Life_Centre "Sea Life Centre") in Great Yarmouth is one of the biggest sea life centres in the country. The Great Yarmouth centre is home to a tropical shark display, one resident of which is Britain's biggest shark 'Nobby' the [Nurse Shark](/wiki/Nurse_Shark "Nurse Shark"). The same display, with its walk-through underwater tunnel, also features the wreckage of a World War II aircraft. The centre also includes over 50 native species including shrimps, starfish, sharks, stingrays and conger eels.
* The [Sea Life Sanctuary](/wiki/Sea_Life_Centres#Sanctuaries "Sea Life Centres") in [Hunstanton](/wiki/Hunstanton "Hunstanton") is Norfolk's leading marine rescue centre and works both as a visitor attraction as well as a location for rescuing and rehabilitating sick and injured sea creatures found in the nearby [Wash](/wiki/The_Wash "The Wash") and [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea"). The attractions main features are similar to that of the Sea Life Centre in Great Yarmouth, albeit on a slightly smaller scale.

Norwich Playhouse

The [Pavilion Theatre](/wiki/Pavilion_Theatre,_Cromer_Pier "Pavilion Theatre, Cromer Pier") (Cromer) is a 510-seater venue on the end of Cromer Pier, best known for hosting the 'end-of-the-pier' show, the Seaside Special. The theatre also presents comedy, music, dance, opera, musicals and community shows.

The Britannia Pier Theatre (Great Yarmouth) mainly hosts popular comedy acts such as the [Chuckle Brothers](/wiki/Chuckle_Brothers "Chuckle Brothers") and [Jim Davidson](/wiki/Jim_Davidson_(comedian) "Jim Davidson (comedian)"). The theatre has 1,200 seats and is one of the largest in Norfolk.

The [Theatre Royal](/wiki/Theatre_Royal,_Norwich "Theatre Royal, Norwich") in Norwich has been on its present site for over 250 years, having been authorised by the [Norwich Theatre Act 1768](/w/index.php?title=Norwich_Theatre_Act_1768&action=edit&redlink=1 "Norwich Theatre Act 1768 (page does not exist)") ([8 Geo. 3](/wiki/8_Geo._3 "8 Geo. 3"). c. 28). The 1,300-seat theatre, the largest in the city, hosts a mix of national touring productions including musicals, dance, drama, family shows, stand-up comedians, opera and pop.

The [Norwich Playhouse](/wiki/Norwich_Playhouse "Norwich Playhouse") hosts theatre, comedy, music and other performing arts. It has a seating capacity of 300.

The [Maddermarket Theatre](/wiki/Maddermarket_Theatre "Maddermarket Theatre") in Norwich opened in 1921 and was the first permanent recreation of an Elizabethan theatre. The founder was [Nugent Monck](/wiki/Walter_Nugent_Monck "Walter Nugent Monck") who had worked with [William Poel](/wiki/William_Poel "William Poel"). The theatre has a seating capacity of 312.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

The [Norwich Puppet Theatre](/wiki/Norwich_Puppet_Theatre "Norwich Puppet Theatre") was founded in 1979 by Ray and Joan DaSilva as a permanent base for their touring company and opened to the public in 1980, following the conversion of the medieval church of St James in central Norwich.[[66]](#cite_note-66) Under subsequent artistic directors, Barry Smith and Luis Z. Boy, the theatre developed its current programme of performances and activities. It is one of the few British theatres dedicated solely to puppetry[[67]](#cite_note-67) and houses a 185-seat raked auditorium, a 50-seat Octagon Studio, workshops, an exhibition gallery, shop, and licensed bar. It remains the only venue in the East of England offering a year-round programme of family-oriented puppet performances.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

The Garage studio theatre (Norwich) can seat up to 110 people in a range of different layouts. It can also be used for standing events and can accommodate up to 180 people.

The Platform Theatre (Norwich) is in the grounds of [City College Norwich](/wiki/City_College_Norwich "City College Norwich") (CCN), and has a large stage with raked seating for an audience of around 200. The theatre plays host to performances by both student and professional companies.

The [Sewell Barn Theatre](/wiki/Sewell_Barn_Theatre "Sewell Barn Theatre") (Norwich) is the smallest theatre in Norwich and has a seating capacity of 100. The auditorium features raked seating on three sides of an open acting space.

The [Norwich Arts Centre](/wiki/Norwich_Arts_Centre "Norwich Arts Centre") (Norwich) theatre opened in 1977 in St. Benedict's Street, and has a capacity of 290.

The [Princess Theatre](/w/index.php?title=Princess_Theatre,_Hunstanton&action=edit&redlink=1 "Princess Theatre, Hunstanton (page does not exist)") (Hunstanton) stands overlooking the Wash and the [green](/wiki/Village_green "Village green") in the East Coast resort of Hunstanton. It is a 472-seat venue. Open all year round, the theatre plays host to a wide variety of shows from comedy to drama, celebrity shows to music for all tastes and children's productions. It has a six-week summer season plus an annual Christmas pantomime.

[Sheringham Little Theatre](/wiki/Sheringham_Little_Theatre "Sheringham Little Theatre") has seating for 180. The theatre programmes a variety of plays, musicals and music, and also shows films.

The [Gorleston Pavilion](/wiki/Gorleston_Pavilion "Gorleston Pavilion") is an original [Edwardian](/wiki/Edwardian_era "Edwardian era") building with a seating capacity of 300, situated on the Norfolk coast. The theatre stages plays, pantomimes, musicals and concerts as well as a 26-week summer season.

## Demography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norfolk&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Demography")]

According to estimates by the [Office for National Statistics](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics"), the population of Norfolk in 2018 was 903,680, split almost evenly between males and females. Roughly 24.3% of the population was aged 65 or older, compared to 18.2% for the whole of England.
